Chronic Wasting Disease (CWD) is `a fatal, contagious neurological illness affecting deer, elk, and moose, caused by infectious prions that trigger weight loss, listlessness, and behavioral changes` . It has spread to over 36 U.S. states and is highly persistent in the environment. No cure exists, making it a critical threat to wild populations, though it is not currently known to infect humans.[[1]](https://www.cdc.gov/chronic-wasting/animals/index.html)[[2]](https://www.usgs.gov/faqs/what-chronic-wasting-disease)[[3]](https://www.courant.com/2026/04/28/this-fast-spreading-disease-is-being-called-the-biggest-threat-to-deer-its-getting-closer-to-ct/)[[4]](https://wildlife.utah.gov/chronic-wasting-disease.html#:~:text=It's%20caused%20by%20prions%2C%20which%20are%20extremely,have%20tested%20positive%20for%20CWD%20in%20Utah.)

**Key Facts About CWD** 

- **Transmission & Symptoms:** CWD spreads through direct animal-to-animal contact or contaminated environments (saliva, urine, feces). Symptoms take roughly 16+ months to appear, including drastic weight loss, stumbling, drooping ears, and lack of fear of humans.
- **Geographic Spread:** As of early 2026, it is reported in at least 36 states, several Canadian provinces, and South Korea.
- **Environmental Persistence:** The causative prions are extremely hardy, remaining infectious in the soil and environment for years.[[1]](https://www.cdc.gov/chronic-wasting/animals/index.html)[[2]](https://www.nps.gov/articles/cwd.htm#:~:text=Transmission%20of%20CWD%20occurs%20through:%20*%20Direct,is%20suspected%20or%20confirmed%20to%20have%20CWD)[[3]](https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/communicable/zoonoses/cwd.htm#:~:text=How%20soon%20after%20CWD%20exposure%20do%20signs,they%20have%20been%20infected%20for%20several%20months.)[[4]](https://www.usgs.gov/faqs/what-chronic-wasting-disease)[[5]](https://wildlife.utah.gov/chronic-wasting-disease.html#:~:text=It's%20caused%20by%20prions%2C%20which%20are%20extremely,have%20tested%20positive%20for%20CWD%20in%20Utah.)[[6]](https://cwd-info.org/cwd-the-basics/)

**Risks and Management** 

- **Human Health:** While there is no strong evidence of CWD infecting humans, the CDC advises against eating meat from infected animals. Hunters are advised to test animals in known CWD areas and avoid consuming brain or spinal cord tissues.
- **Prevention Measures:** Wildlife agencies advise against baiting or feeding deer, which causes unnatural congregation and increases spread. Hunters are encouraged to follow local carcass transport regulations to prevent moving the disease to new areas.
- **Testing:** Surveillance is crucial. Hunters can contribute to management by utilizing state-run testing stations, such as those provided by the [California Department of Fish and Wildlife](https://wildlife.ca.gov/Conservation/Laboratories/Wildlife-Health/Monitoring/CWD) or [MassWildlife](https://www.mass.gov/info-details/chronic-wasting-disease-cwd).[[1]](https://www.cdc.gov/chronic-wasting/animals/index.html)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chronic_wasting_disease#:~:text=The%20epidemiological%20study%20further%20concluded%2C%20%22as%20a,from%20areas%20where%20CWD%20has%20been%20identified%22.)[[3]](https://www.mass.gov/info-details/chronic-wasting-disease-cwd)[[4]](https://wdfw.wa.gov/species-habitats/diseases/chronic-wasting/what-is-cwd)[[5]](https://wildlife.ca.gov/Conservation/Laboratories/Wildlife-Health/Monitoring/CWD)

For the most up-to-date information on the disease, the [Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)](https://www.cdc.gov/chronic-wasting/animals/index.html) offers detailed, actionable information.[[1]](https://www.cdc.gov/chronic-wasting/animals/index.html)[[2]](https://nwco.net/training-modules/wildlife-diseases/#:~:text=One%20extremely%20valuable%20source%20for%20current%20and,language%20and%20get%20right%20to%20the%20point.)

items.text: Chronic Wasting Disease (CWD) is caused by misfolded proteins called prions, which infect deer, elk, and moose, causing a fatal, degenerative brain disease. These prions are spread directly between animals or indirectly through contaminated environments, saliva, urine, or feces. Prions are highly resistant, remaining in the environment for years.

items.title: Key Causes and Transmission Factors:
items.text: Prion Infection: The infectious prions induce normal proteins in the brain and other tissues to misfold, creating lesions and sponge-like brain tissue. Direct Transmission: Spread through saliva, blood, urine, or fecal contact between animals. Environmental Contamination: Prions can stay in soil and water for years, infecting healthy animals that graze in contaminated areas. Animal Density: Feeding sites, salt licks, and areas where animals concentrate facilitate faster spread.

items.text: The prognosis for animals with Chronic Wasting Disease (CWD) is 100% fatal, as it is a progressive, incurable, and untreatable neurological prion disease. Infected cervids (deer, elk, moose) appear healthy for 18–30 months, but once clinical signs appear, they die within a few weeks to months.

items.title: Usage Examples & Clinical Prognosis
items.text: Irreversible Decline: Once symptoms like dramatic weight loss, droopy ears, excessive salivation, or lack of fear appear, death is certain, typically within 4 months of onset. Long Asymptomatic Phase: Animals act normally while transmitting the prions through saliva, feces, and urine before symptoms appear. Population Impacts: The disease lowers survival rates and can drive local population declines, as no immunity exists.

items.text: Always Fatal: The disease is invariably fatal. "Zombie Deer Disease": A popular, though sometimes inaccurate, term used for the behavioral changes (e.g., staggering, confusion). Transmissible Spongiform Encephalopathy (TSE): The scientific classification, similar to Mad Cow disease, describing the sponge-like holes formed in the brain. Debilitating Syndrome: Known for producing severe wasting or emaciation in its final stages.

items.text: Chronic Wasting Disease (CWD) is transmitted among cervids (deer, elk, moose) via direct animal-to-animal contact or indirect environmental contamination from prions in saliva, urine, feces, and carcass decomposition. These highly persistent prions contaminate soil, food, and water sources, allowing transmission to continue even without direct contact.

items.title: Key Transmission Routes and Details:
items.text: Direct Transmission: The infection spreads directly through contact with saliva, feces, urine, blood, and carcasses of infected animals. Indirect Transmission: Prions contaminate the environment, specifically soil and water, where they remain infectious for long periods. Vertical Transmission: Evidence suggests infected mothers can pass the disease to their offspring. Species Affected: It primarily affects cervids (deer, elk, reindeer, sika deer, and moose). Environmental Persistence: The infectious prions are resistant to conventional inactivation methods. Movement: Spread is linked to both natural migration routes, often along waterways, and human-facilitated transportation of infected captive animals.

items.text: Chronic Wasting Disease (CWD) is a fatal, contagious prion disease in cervids (deer, elk, moose) with no known cure or vaccine. Prevention relies on stopping the spread of infected carcasses, managing animal density, restricting artificial feeding, and proper disposal of remains.

items.title: Key Prevention and Management Strategies:
items.text: Restrict Carcass Movement: Avoid transporting whole carcasses from CWD-affected areas to new locations; transporting carcasses is a major source of spreading the disease, as prions can remain in the environment for years. Safe Carcass Disposal: Dispose of deer remains properly, such as burying them deeply or using approved landfills, rather than leaving them on the landscape. Prohibit Feeding/Baiting: Stop feeding or baiting deer, as this artificially concentrates animals in one area, significantly increasing the risk of disease transmission. Test Harvested Animals: Submit deer and elk heads for testing in areas known to have CWD. Manage Animal Density: Control cervid population density through regulated hunting to slow the natural spread of the disease. Avoid Contaminated Attractants: Use synthetic lures rather than natural deer urine-based lures, which may contain CWD prions. Field Handling Safety: Wear gloves when dressing game and avoid handling organs, particularly the brain and spinal cord, especially in known CWD zones.

Epizootic Hemorrhagic Disease (EHD) is a viral disease, `often fatal to white-tailed deer` , spread by biting midges in late summer/early fall, particularly during droughts. Symptoms include high fever, weakness, excessive salivation, and swelling of the head/neck, often leading deer to congregate near water. While mortality can be high, localized herds usually rebound within 2–3 years.[[1]](https://www.in.gov/dnr/fish-and-wildlife/wildlife-resources/wildlife-diseases-in-indiana/epizootic-hemorrhagic-disease-ehd/)[[2]](https://cwhl.vet.cornell.edu/resource/hemorrhagic-disease-deer)[[3]](https://idfg.idaho.gov/article/ehd-recurring-disease-and-unfortunate-reality-idahos-white-tailed-deer)[[4]](https://www.northamericanwhitetail.com/editorial/backup-plan-against-ehd/385459#:~:text=Animals%20that%20survive%20the%20chronic%20form%20of,deer%20littered%20creek%20beds%2C%20sloughs%2C%20and%20waterways)

**Symptoms of EHD in Deer** 

- **Behavioral Changes:** Loss of appetite, loss of fear of humans, weakness, and lameness.
- **Physical Signs:** High fever causing them to seek cool water (often found dead in/near creeks).
- **Appearance:** Swelling of the head, neck, tongue, or eyelids; excessive salivation; bluish tongue (hence the nickname "blue tongue").
- **Rapid Progression:** Death usually occurs within 5 to 10 days of infection.[[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IZS-yxQ3kqc#:~:text=EHD%20in%20deer%20is%20spread%20by%20biting,have%20high%20temps%20&%20go%20to%20water.)[[2]](https://idfg.idaho.gov/article/ehd-recurring-disease-and-unfortunate-reality-idahos-white-tailed-deer)[[3]](https://www.northamericanwhitetail.com/editorial/backup-plan-against-ehd/385459#:~:text=Animals%20that%20survive%20the%20chronic%20form%20of,deer%20littered%20creek%20beds%2C%20sloughs%2C%20and%20waterways)[[4]](https://cwhl.vet.cornell.edu/resource/hemorrhagic-disease-deer)[[5]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/ZgB7kWQ4gJI)

**Impact on Deer and Populations** 

- **Mortality:** EHD can cause significant, rapid death rates in local white-tailed deer populations.
- **Survival:** Some deer do survive and develop immunity.
- **Herd Recovery:** Despite high mortality, herds often recover within two to three years.
- **Seasonality:** The disease typically ends abruptly after the first hard frost kills the midge vectors.[[1]](https://www.in.gov/boah/species-information/cervids-deer-elk/epizootic-hemorrhagic-disease-ehd/)[[2]](https://idfg.idaho.gov/article/ehd-recurring-disease-and-unfortunate-reality-idahos-white-tailed-deer)[[3]](https://www.northamericanwhitetail.com/editorial/backup-plan-against-ehd/385459#:~:text=Animals%20that%20survive%20the%20chronic%20form%20of,deer%20littered%20creek%20beds%2C%20sloughs%2C%20and%20waterways)[[4]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xmNs15FT28k#:~:text=Hemorrhagic%20disease%2C%20a%20family%20of%20viruses%20including,agency%20wildlife%20biologist%20with%20a%20precise%20location.)

**Management and Risks** 

- **Transmission:** EHD cannot be transmitted directly from deer to deer; it requires the midge vector.
- **Human Risk:** EHD does not affect humans, but it is advised not to consume sick animals.
- **Action:** If you find dead deer, particularly near water in late summer, report it to the nearest state agency wildlife biologist.[[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xmNs15FT28k#:~:text=Hemorrhagic%20disease%2C%20a%20family%20of%20viruses%20including,agency%20wildlife%20biologist%20with%20a%20precise%20location.)[[2]](https://www.instagram.com/reel/DOgEaEfDaT9/)[[3]](https://www.in.gov/boah/species-information/cervids-deer-elk/epizootic-hemorrhagic-disease-ehd/)[[4]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IZS-yxQ3kqc#:~:text=EHD%20in%20deer%20is%20spread%20by%20biting,have%20high%20temps%20&%20go%20to%20water.)

For more information, you can visit resources from the [Washington Department of Fish & Wildlife](https://wdfw.wa.gov/species-habitats/diseases/hemorrhagic) and the Pennsylvania Game Commission.[[1]](https://wdfw.wa.gov/species-habitats/diseases/hemorrhagic#:~:text=If%20you%20see%20a%20deer%2C%20elk%2C%20or,Wildlife%20%29%20's%20wildlife%20observations%20reporting%20tool.)

items.markdown: Epizootic Hemorrhagic Disease (EHD) is a viral disease, `often fatal to white-tailed deer` , spread by biting midges in late summer/early fall, particularly during droughts. Symptoms include high fever, weakness, excessive salivation, and swelling of the head/neck, often leading deer to congregate near water. While mortality can be high, localized herds usually rebound within 2–3 years.[[1]](https://www.in.gov/dnr/fish-and-wildlife/wildlife-resources/wildlife-diseases-in-indiana/epizootic-hemorrhagic-disease-ehd/)[[2]](https://cwhl.vet.cornell.edu/resource/hemorrhagic-disease-deer)[[3]](https://idfg.idaho.gov/article/ehd-recurring-disease-and-unfortunate-reality-idahos-white-tailed-deer)[[4]](https://www.northamericanwhitetail.com/editorial/backup-plan-against-ehd/385459#:~:text=Animals%20that%20survive%20the%20chronic%20form%20of,deer%20littered%20creek%20beds%2C%20sloughs%2C%20and%20waterways)

items.title: Symptoms of EHD in Deer
items.text: Behavioral Changes: Loss of appetite, loss of fear of humans, weakness, and lameness. Physical Signs: High fever causing them to seek cool water (often found dead in/near creeks). Appearance: Swelling of the head, neck, tongue, or eyelids; excessive salivation; bluish tongue (hence the nickname "blue tongue"). Rapid Progression: Death usually occurs within 5 to 10 days of infection.
items.markdown:
- **Behavioral Changes:** Loss of appetite, loss of fear of humans, weakness, and lameness.
- **Physical Signs:** High fever causing them to seek cool water (often found dead in/near creeks).
- **Appearance:** Swelling of the head, neck, tongue, or eyelids; excessive salivation; bluish tongue (hence the nickname "blue tongue").
- **Rapid Progression:** Death usually occurs within 5 to 10 days of infection.[[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IZS-yxQ3kqc#:~:text=EHD%20in%20deer%20is%20spread%20by%20biting,have%20high%20temps%20&%20go%20to%20water.)[[2]](https://idfg.idaho.gov/article/ehd-recurring-disease-and-unfortunate-reality-idahos-white-tailed-deer)[[3]](https://www.northamericanwhitetail.com/editorial/backup-plan-against-ehd/385459#:~:text=Animals%20that%20survive%20the%20chronic%20form%20of,deer%20littered%20creek%20beds%2C%20sloughs%2C%20and%20waterways)[[4]](https://cwhl.vet.cornell.edu/resource/hemorrhagic-disease-deer)[[5]](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/ZgB7kWQ4gJI)

items.title: Impact on Deer and Populations
items.text: Mortality: EHD can cause significant, rapid death rates in local white-tailed deer populations. Survival: Some deer do survive and develop immunity. Herd Recovery: Despite high mortality, herds often recover within two to three years. Seasonality: The disease typically ends abruptly after the first hard frost kills the midge vectors.
items.markdown:
- **Mortality:** EHD can cause significant, rapid death rates in local white-tailed deer populations.
- **Survival:** Some deer do survive and develop immunity.
- **Herd Recovery:** Despite high mortality, herds often recover within two to three years.
- **Seasonality:** The disease typically ends abruptly after the first hard frost kills the midge vectors.[[1]](https://www.in.gov/boah/species-information/cervids-deer-elk/epizootic-hemorrhagic-disease-ehd/)[[2]](https://idfg.idaho.gov/article/ehd-recurring-disease-and-unfortunate-reality-idahos-white-tailed-deer)[[3]](https://www.northamericanwhitetail.com/editorial/backup-plan-against-ehd/385459#:~:text=Animals%20that%20survive%20the%20chronic%20form%20of,deer%20littered%20creek%20beds%2C%20sloughs%2C%20and%20waterways)[[4]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xmNs15FT28k#:~:text=Hemorrhagic%20disease%2C%20a%20family%20of%20viruses%20including,agency%20wildlife%20biologist%20with%20a%20precise%20location.)

items.title: Management and Risks
items.text: Transmission: EHD cannot be transmitted directly from deer to deer; it requires the midge vector. Human Risk: EHD does not affect humans, but it is advised not to consume sick animals. Action: If you find dead deer, particularly near water in late summer, report it to the nearest state agency wildlife biologist.
items.markdown:
- **Transmission:** EHD cannot be transmitted directly from deer to deer; it requires the midge vector.
- **Human Risk:** EHD does not affect humans, but it is advised not to consume sick animals.
- **Action:** If you find dead deer, particularly near water in late summer, report it to the nearest state agency wildlife biologist.[[1]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xmNs15FT28k#:~:text=Hemorrhagic%20disease%2C%20a%20family%20of%20viruses%20including,agency%20wildlife%20biologist%20with%20a%20precise%20location.)[[2]](https://www.instagram.com/reel/DOgEaEfDaT9/)[[3]](https://www.in.gov/boah/species-information/cervids-deer-elk/epizootic-hemorrhagic-disease-ehd/)[[4]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IZS-yxQ3kqc#:~:text=EHD%20in%20deer%20is%20spread%20by%20biting,have%20high%20temps%20&%20go%20to%20water.)
